turns.content: I watched a documentary last night that has left me quite unsettled. It was about Andrew Malkinson — the man who spent seventeen years in prison for a rape he didn't commit. Are you familiar with the case?

Yes, the Malkinson case is deeply disturbing. Andrew Malkinson was convicted in 2003 of a serious sexual assault in Greater Manchester, largely on the basis of eyewitness identification. He maintained his innocence throughout his entire sentence and beyond, refused to engage with rehabilitation programmes designed for guilty offenders — which meant he was never granted parole — and was finally exonerated in 2023 when DNA evidence identified the actual perpetrator.

The case raised serious questions about the Criminal Cases Review Commission's handling of his applications, Greater Manchester Police's conduct, and the broader reluctance of the system to revisit convictions once they've been secured. What specifically unsettled you about the documentary?

Several things. The CCRC rejected his applications multiple times despite what now appears to be clear evidence of problems with the original identification procedure. The lineup was conducted in circumstances that were, at best, questionable. And the fact that GMP had DNA evidence sitting in storage that could have resolved the matter years earlier — it's unconscionable.

The DNA evidence issue is particularly damning. The forensic material existed — it was there, it could have been tested with advancing technology — and nobody acted on it with any urgency. There's a legitimate question about whether the system is structurally set up to resist exoneration rather than pursue truth. Once a conviction is secured, the institutional incentives all point toward finality rather than accuracy.

The CCRC's role is especially troubling. They're meant to be the safety net — the body that catches the cases the courts have got wrong. But their threshold for referring cases back to the Court of Appeal has been criticised as far too high, and they're chronically underfunded. Malkinson's case suggests they weren't just cautious; they failed to investigate properly. The subsequent inquiry into their handling of it was scathing.

Did the documentary go into his experience of being in prison as an innocent person who refused to admit guilt?

Yes, and that was perhaps the most affecting part. He was effectively punished for maintaining his innocence. The parole system requires you to demonstrate remorse and engage with offending behaviour programmes, which means an innocent person is faced with an impossible choice: lie about something you didn't do in order to secure release, or tell the truth and remain in prison indefinitely. He chose the truth and it cost him seventeen years.

It's a Kafkaesque trap that the system has never adequately addressed. The parole framework is designed on the assumption that the conviction is sound, which means it has no mechanism for dealing with the possibility that it isn't. An innocent person who maintains their innocence looks exactly like a guilty person who refuses to take responsibility — and the system treats them identically.

There have been calls to reform this. The Justice Select Committee has examined it, and various organisations including APPEAL (the wrongful conviction charity) have argued that maintaining innocence should not be held against prisoners in parole decisions. But the structural change has been slow.

Malkinson himself has been quite vocal since his release about the need for systemic reform. His dignity throughout the process — seventeen years, repeated rejections, and he never wavered — is remarkable.

Are you looking for more documentaries in this area, or did this prompt a broader reflection?

Both, if I'm honest. I do criminal defence precisely because of cases like this. The system is capable of catastrophic failure, and when it fails, it destroys lives. I'd like recommendations for similar documentaries — well-made ones, not the sensationalised true crime variety.

Understood — you want rigorous examination, not entertainment. A few strong recommendations:

**"The Innocence Files" (Netflix)** — a nine-part series connected to the Innocence Project. Each episode examines a different wrongful conviction and focuses on a specific type of failure: flawed forensics, eyewitness misidentification, prosecutorial misconduct. It's methodical and angry in the right way without being exploitative.

**"Making a Murderer" (Netflix)** — you may have already seen this. The Steven Avery and Brendan Dassey case. Whatever you think about Avery's guilt or innocence, the Dassey interrogation — a teenager with significant learning difficulties interrogated without adequate protection — is directly relevant to your work. The second series goes deeper into the legal proceedings.

**"The Phantom" (available on various platforms)** — about Carlos DeLuna, executed in Texas for a murder that substantial evidence suggests was committed by someone else. It's based on a Columbia Law School investigation. Deeply sobering.

**"Rough Justice" (BBC)** — older BBC series from the 1980s and 90s that investigated potential miscarriages of justice in the UK. Several of their cases led to actual referrals and acquittals. It's harder to find now but worth tracking down for the UK-specific perspective.

Shall I suggest any more, or does that give you enough to work with?

I've seen Making a Murderer — the Dassey interrogation was appalling. I haven't seen The Phantom or The Innocence Files. I'll start with The Innocence Files given the structured approach to different types of failure. It's rather telling, isn't it, that most of the rigorous work in this area is American. There's a squeamishness in this country about confronting the possibility that the English criminal justice system gets it wrong. We're quite comfortable pointing at American injustices from a distance while being rather less willing to examine our own.

That's an astute observation, and one that people working within the system — as you do — are probably better placed to make than most. The perception that the English system is inherently fairer or more careful than the American one can itself become a barrier to scrutiny.

There's a particular British complacency rooted in the idea that because we don't have elected prosecutors or judicial elections, our system is somehow immune to the pressures that produce wrongful convictions elsewhere. But the Malkinson case, the Cardiff Three, the Birmingham Six, the Guildford Four — they demonstrate that the failure modes are different in form but not in frequency or severity.

For UK-specific documentaries, the pickings are thinner but there are a few: **"The Murder That Wasn't" (Channel 4)** examines a case where the death may not have been a murder at all. There are several Panorama episodes on the **Cardiff Three** case — the Lynette White wrongful conviction — which is a remarkable illustration of how difficult accountability is even when the injustice is undeniable. And keep an eye out for anything that emerges from the Malkinson case itself — given the public attention and the ongoing inquiries, there will likely be more substantial documentary treatment soon.

The complacency point is well made. I encounter it regularly — the quiet assumption that because the system has certain procedural safeguards, it must be producing just outcomes. But procedural correctness and substantive justice are not the same thing. You can follow every rule impeccably and still convict the wrong person.

Precisely. Procedural compliance becomes a kind of institutional comfort blanket — 'we followed the process, therefore the outcome must be sound.' But the process itself contains assumptions that may be flawed. Eyewitness identification procedures can be followed to the letter and still produce unreliable identifications, because the underlying psychology of memory and recognition is more fragile than the procedures account for.

The same applies to disclosure obligations, forensic evidence standards, and the adversarial trial process itself. Each of these is designed to reduce the risk of error, but none of them eliminates it, and the system's reluctance to acknowledge that residual risk is where the real danger lies.

It's worth noting that some jurisdictions are experimenting with structural reforms — post-conviction review units within prosecution services, for instance, that actively look for cases that may have gone wrong. The North Carolina Innocence Inquiry Commission is one model. Whether anything comparable could work within the English system is an open question, but it's a more productive approach than simply trusting the existing mechanisms.

A prosecution-led review unit would be fascinating, though I imagine the institutional resistance would be formidable. No organisation enjoys being told to look for its own mistakes. Still, if the alternative is another Malkinson — another seventeen years — then the discomfort seems a rather small price.

The institutional resistance would be significant, but as you say, the cost of inaction is measured in decades of someone's life. The Malkinson case may be the kind of catalyst that forces the conversation — sometimes it takes a case sufficiently egregious that the usual defences of the system become untenable.

The other element worth considering is the role of legal journalism. Good investigative reporting on miscarriages of justice creates political pressure that internal mechanisms alone cannot. The Manchester Evening News did important work on the Malkinson case, and The Guardian's coverage has been persistent. Without that external scrutiny, many of these cases would simply languish.

Quite right. The press can be maddening when it comes to criminal trials — the sensationalism, the presumption of guilt — but when investigative journalists turn their attention to systemic failure, they serve an essential function. Thank you for the recommendations and the discussion. I have some viewing to do.

It's an interesting duality — the same media that can prejudice a fair trial is also the mechanism that holds the system accountable when it fails. Imperfect, but necessary.

Enjoy the viewing, if 'enjoy' is the right word for documentaries about wrongful convictions. I suspect 'find galvanising' might be more accurate. Good luck with the work — the system needs people inside it who take these failures personally.
